speaker: interviewer
text: We're in the home of Hedy Epstein. It's the 7th of December, 1995, in St. Louis, Missouri, the Chicago region, USA. Tape two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hedy, you were explaining what happened the morning following Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Hans came into my classroom because he'd had pretty much the same experience as I did. And maybe because I was a year older than he, he thought I was also a year wiser.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked me, you know, what are we going to do? And I said Hans, you go back to your classroom, sit at your desk, and study. And he said, no, I'm scared. Can I stay here? And I said, all right, you can stay here, but you have to be quiet because I'm studying. And then Hans said, well can I go over and look out the window? And I said, yes, you can do that. But you must be quiet now because I'm studying. And Hans stood by the window, I don't know, maybe an hour or an hour and a half when he all off the sudden became very excited and he said, come here. Look. Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: And what we saw were men and boys being marched down the street, four in a row, chained to each other, chained to the ones in front of them, chained to the ones behind them, with SS men hitting them with whips and urging them to walk faster. We didn't know who they were because the school sat back from the road quite a bit. But we somehow knew, even though we didn't know who they were, that they are Jewish. And with that, we decided we'd better call home and find out what's going on. And we went next door to a bookstore where there was a phone. And I called my mother. And a strange voice answered and said, the phone is no longer in service. I then called my father at his place of business and my grandmother and my aunt. And I was told the phone is no longer in service. Hans called his mother and some other people. And he too was told, the phone is no longer in service. With that, we decided that maybe we should go home, though we were afraid. It was, you know, midmorning, and you don't just walk out of school without asking anyone. But there also was no one there that we could ask. And so we went home. And as I approached my home, I noticed it looked different than the way it looked in the morning. We had green shutters in our house. And they were never closed during the daytime. And they weren't closed that morning. Sometimes in the winter if it was very and very windy, we'd close them at night. And when I came back to the house that morning, they were closed. I ran to the door and it was locked. And I didn't even know that you could lock it. I rang the doorbell. And I could hear it, but no one answered. And so I stood in front of the house for a couple of minutes trying to figure out, what is going on today. Nothing seems to make a whole lot of sense. When I saw this man walking down the street who I knew was one of the village's worst Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And any other time if I was on the same side of the street as he, I'd cross over to the other side because I was afraid of him. But that morning, in my dismay, I went over to him and I said, do you know where my mother is? And he said, I don't know where the goddamn bitch is. But if I find her, and if she's still alive, I will kill her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And with that, I just took off. And on my way I had to pass the Jewish hardware store. The two display windows were broken. There were people milling around, laughing and joking, and reaching into the store and taking stuff out which I also did not understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I approached my aunt's house, I could see my mother and my aunt looking out the window. And I wondered, you know, what are they looking at? They surely don't want to see what I just saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother opened the door. And she looked kind of grotesque. She was quite a bit taller and thinner than my aunt Kaethe, that's K-A-E-T-H-E. And she was wearing my aunt's clothes. We exchanged what happened to each other since we'd last seen each other a couple hours earlier that day. And my mother said that about 10 minutes after I left for school, some Nazis came to the house and they arrested my father. He was still in his pajamas. They did not give him permission to get dressed or even to put a coat on on this very cold day. And as he was being walked out the door, he said to my mother, try to find Kaethe and try to stay together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A couple of the Nazis stayed behind. And they broke the windows in our building and did some other vandalism, broke furniture and dishes and so on. After they left, my mother closed the shutters and secured the building, ran down the street to my aunt's house, not realizing she was still wearing her nightgown. And that's why my aunt gave her some clothes. The reason they were looking out of the window is they had heard that all Jewish men and boys, Jewish boys beginning with the age of 16, had been arrested earlier that morning and had been taken to village hall. And you could see village hall in the next block from my aunt's house. And so they were looking out the window hoping to see the men and boys come back out. And so I joined them at the window. It wasn't long after that that we saw people coming out of village hall. And the group began to march down the street right past my aunt's house. And in that group was my father and my uncle and many other men and boys that I knew. And my mother practically hung me out of the second story window and called out to my father. We have Hedy. We're together. Whether my father heard or saw me, we didn't know. And we watched them until they went around the bend in the road. And then they were out of sight.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where were they going? When will they be coming back? There were no answers to that. And so we closed the window. And we just sat there. When we heard loud knocking on the door downstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: When my uncle Oskar, O-S-K-A-R, was arrested earlier that morning, they didn't do any vandalism to the building. But knowing what had happened to our building, we thought maybe now they're coming to do whatever it is they're going to do. And so my aunt and my mother and I ran up into the attic. And we sat in an old wardrobe up there, or we're hiding, actually, in that wardrobe. And I remember whispering to my mother, I want to get out of here. And not just out of this wardrobe, but I want to get out of Germany. And I guess the reason I said I want to get out of Germany is because I knew that my parents had tried but had not been successful in leaving Germany. And I don't really know how long we sat in that wardrobe. It seemed at the time it was a lifetime. When it was quiet for a while, we decided that it was probably safe to come down. Actually they had done very little damage to my aunt's house. We later learned that all the actions against Jews on that day in our village had been called to a halt at noon. And they must have come to my aunt's house maybe a minute or two before noon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were a number of other things that happened that day. Jewish businesses, for instance, were permanently closed. If you were Jewish and a doctor, a dentist, or an attorney, you weren't allowed to practice. Christian doctors weren't allowed to treat Jews. Jews weren't allowed to go to a hospital. In other words, a lot of the civil rights that people today, that I today, take for granted were taken away from Jews and were taken away permanently. Most of the synagogues all over Germany and Austria, and Austria by that time had already been annexed to Germany, were burned. They did burn the synagogue, or they started to set the synagogue on fire in our village, but very quickly extinguished the fire because the buildings on either side of the synagogue were Christian homes and were very close by. And they were afraid that they might catch fire. But they vandalized the synagogue. For instance, on top of the synagogue there were the two tablets of Moses. And they were torn down. They vandalized the inside, broke the windows. I learned subsequently that they took the Torah scrolls out of the synagogue. And the men that we had seen marching down the street were taken to the next community to the railroad station there. And by the time they arrived there, the Torah scrolls were strung up open on the platform for them to see. I was so traumatized by the events of that day, that I did not permit my mother or my aunt out of my sight. For instance, if one of us had to go to the bathroom, three of us went to the bathroom. We could not get the windows replaced in our home, so we decided to stay at my aunt's house. And we moved an extra bed into the bedroom so the three of us could sleep in the same room because I would not sleep alone in a room. There was absolutely no information about the men and boys who had been taken away for two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hedy, how did you continue to have food to eat and money?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some food, of course, in my aunt's house. And we had some in our house. And while I don't remember it, I imagine we got the food from our house back over to my aunt's house. And we had some money, though the money-- and I don't quite know how that was-- but I know the money was confiscated. And we were given an allowance every month. And I don't know what the amount was on which we lived. I don't really recall how that all happened and how we got the food. I know there was food. I wasn't hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess it wasn't really that important to me. The thing that really troubled me is, where is my father? Where is my uncle? Where are all the men and boys? My whole life had changed. Also another thing that happened was Jewish children were no longer able to attend either public or private schools. So, you know, I had all this time on my hands. And I guess I was-- the main thing that I can remember is being afraid, terribly afraid. I mean, if my mother moved from one chair to the other, where is she going? How did you spend your time all those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, that's a good question. But I cannot answer it. I just-- all I remember is fear. And I think I was probably paralyzed. I mean, not physically, but emotionally paralyzed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I don't remember doing anything except fear, fear. Where is my father? Is he still alive? Is he going to come back? Where is my uncle? What happened to my other uncles? What happened to my grandfather? We didn't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was no information about these men and boys who had been arrested and taken away for two weeks. And then within a matter of a day or two, preprinted postcards arrived from the concentration camp Dachau, D-A-C-H-A-U. Dachau, by the way, was the first concentration camp that the Nazis constructed in 1933. By November 1938 there were many others. And Jews who lived in different sections of Germany were sent to some of the other concentration camps. All the Jews from where I lived sent to Dachau. And there was a-- half of a postcard was preprinted. And it gave all the dos and don'ts, that you can't come and visit, that efforts to release the men would be futile, how often you can write, how much you can write, and so on and so forth. Once we knew where the men were, where my father was, where all the other men were, my mother decided to go to the Gestapo office, that's G-E-S-T-A-P-O, that's the Nazi secret police office which was in Karlsruhe, that's K-A-R-L-S-R-U-H-E, a town that was quite some distance from where we lived. And my mother left early in the morning by train and came back late at night by train. And I remember begging my mother not to go. And I said, you know, because if they keep you, I won't have a mother and I won't have a father. But my mother said, you want your father and all the others to come home. And I have to go and see if there's anything that I can do to bring that about. And so you must let me go. And so reluctantly I let her go and lived in fear until she came back late at night. And she went every day and came back with no information though, until the Monday of the fourth week after the men and boys had been arrested. And she was told that morning that if-- that my father would come back that week. But if he's not back by Friday, he will never come back anymore because he's no longer alive. And the first few men arrived back on that very day. And we heard about it. And we visited them. But I was not really allowed to remain in the room when conversation took place. My mother said, you're still too young. But one thing made an indelible impression on me. Every man and every boy that came back had his hair completely shaved off. And I had never seen anyone look that way. On Tuesday morning, we went back to our house because naturally my father would come there. But he did not come back on Tuesday and not on Wednesday nor on Thursday. And on Friday morning, I think my mother temporarily lost some of her sanity because she absolutely refused to get out of bed. She said, my husband is dead. I don't want to get out of bed. I don't want to go on living anymore. And my aunt and I tried to prevail upon her how important it is for her to get up and go back to our house. But she just would not get up. And so I asked my aunt to accompany me back to our house. And my aunt said, I can't leave you mother in the condition that she's in. You go by yourself. And I said, no I'm scared. And just about then we heard a knock on the door downstairs. And for the first time in four weeks I did something on my own. I went to the window and looked out and I thought I recognized my father. And I called out, Daddy's home. And my mother said no, no, no. It's a ruse. It's the Nazis. Let's go back up in the attic and hide in the wardrobe. But I went downstairs. And I opened the door and indeed it was my father. And when he was arrested they took him away in his pajamas. They had given him clothes, including a hat. And when I opened the door, he removed his hat. And I said, oh my God, they shaved your hair. I had seen all the other men with their shaved heads. But this was my father. And I thought he was different. But he really wasn't. And he was so ashamed when I said this, that he not only put his hat back on, but he pulled it practically all the way down to his chin. It took quite a while before my mother realized that the man standing next to her bed is her husband, is my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: I noticed that my father's hands were in just really bad shape. And I said to him, what did they do to your hands? And he said, it isn't so much what they did to my hands, but Dachau was very, very cold. And I had no gloves. So I have frostbites on my hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also sometimes I had to go to the kitchen and pick up this great, big cauldron of hot soup and carry it back to the barracks for my barrack mates. And some of it spilled. And so I probably have some burns on my hands. But now that I'm home, it'll heal. So don't worry about it. Once my mother came around and realized that her husband had come home, she urged him to change his clothes, to take a bath. And he was very reluctant to do so. And he explained, when I was in Dachau I was beaten. And my body is very sore and swollen. And these clothes fit really tight. If I'm going to take them off, it's going to hurt. And so my mother-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Oh shoot. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My mother got a pair of scissors and cut open the sleeves on his jacket and sort of peeled him out of the jacket. They then went to the bathroom. I did not see his body. So I don't know how bad it looked. But later that day my father had what was probably a mild heart attack. And remember I said earlier that Jewish doctors weren't allowed to practice. And there wasn't a Jewish doctor in the village anyway. And Christian doctors weren't allowed to practice-- I mean, weren't allowed to treat Jews. But my mother got word to our doctor, who was Christian. And this man took tremendous risk. By the way, his name was Dr. Bruno, B-R-U-N-O, Weber, W-E-B-E-R. He came late at night to our home and he treated my father until he was well again. Now, we lived in the village. And everyone knew who everybody is and where everybody lived. So this man literally risked his life because had he been seen, who knows what would've happened to him. He would have been sent to a concentration camp, lost his life. Who knows what would've happened to his family. I just want to give a little aside about this doctor. In 1990 when I was back in the village, I was looking for him because I wanted to thank him and learned that he's no longer alive. But I found his sister. And I talked to her and thanked her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Someone listened to that conversation and took me aside later on and said, you don't know the whole story about this doctor, do you? And I said no, what is the rest of the story? And I was told that it is believed that later on he became one of several Nazi doctors who performed medical experiments on concentration camp inmates.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back to St. Louis, I did some research. And lo and behold I found documentary evidence that would substantiate that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Even today as I'm talking, I find it very difficult to reconcile the image I have of this doctor who risked his life to take care of my father and possibly other people too, and what he did later on. But back to 1938. Once my father was well again, the efforts to leave Germany were resumed. But the focus was changed a bit. Before we tried to leave as a family unit. It was then decided that if one of us has the opportunity to leave, then that person will leave and hopefully the other's can follow. And the opportunity presented itself for me to leave on a children's transport on May 18, 1939. And we left for England. There were 500 of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you left, what were you told, Hedy?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents tried to make this as an-- tell me this is like an adventure. That it was a good learning experience. That I was going to learn a new language, English. That I would be able to go back to school, because I had not been able to be in school since Crystal Night. That I was going to live in a big city, because I knew that I would be living in London. That there's a subway there. And I had never seen the subway or been on a subway before. And they just painted a very exciting, rosy picture. And that it probably wouldn't be long before they would join me in England. Or maybe if we're lucky enough, we will all be able to go to the United States, and that this was just a temporary thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the last conversation you had with your parents before leaving to go to the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. My father and I for many years prior to that would on Sunday morning take a walk together. Sometimes we would bicycle together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sometimes all three of us would go on a day's hike. But on those Sundays when we didn't take a hike together, my father and I would always walk or ride our bicycles. And I would be given a topic to discuss. Like my father would tell me what he wanted me to think about or read for the following Sunday. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I remember [BACKGROUND NOISE] that I always thought about it. I looked up or asked other people to give me information. And the last Sunday before I left, my father and I-- or the Sunday before that, I should say actually. Two Sundays before I left, my father gave me the topic of the immortality of the junebug.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I instantly knew that this was to be my sex education. And I really didn't know very much. But I somehow took this strange attitude that my father wanted to find out from me about sex, and why should I tell him? Let him figure it out himself. And so when we took our walk where I was to discuss this, my father said, well, the topic today as you know is the immortality of the junebug. Have you thought about it or done some reading? No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why not? I just didn't. Well, then he said, well what does immortality mean? It means they don't die out. Well, why don't they die out? Well, they have babies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that conversation went on with him asking me questions and my reluctantly answering him because I did not want to inform him about sex or reproduction. And I sensed his discomfort. And pretty soon the conversation ended about that topic. And we went on and talked about other things. One of the things that I wanted to mention too about these walks that we did, especially in the spring, summer, and fall, we'd go up in the woods and in one place it was this huge meadow and there were all these wild flowers there. And I'd always pick a bouquet of flowers to take home to my mother. And in 1990 when I was back in the village, I took a hike in the woods with my son who accompanied me. And I picked a bouquet of flowers, except unfortunately, I had no one to bring them to. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But back to 1939, to the children's transport. There were 500 of us. The youngest were twins, six months old. And I still remember this young couple handing these children to someone on the train and saying, please take good care of our two babies. The oldest of us were 17. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was 14 and a half at the time. I also remember just before I left some feelings that I had and that I shared with my parents, which must've been extremely painful for my parents. I told them that you probably adopted me and now you no longer want me and you're sending me away. I had the vision or the image that I was a gypsy child. Gypsies used to come to our village once or twice a year and pitch their tent. And the story was gypsies steal children, which really to this day, that bothers me that this is the story about gypsies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: I disconnected the phone.
